Advance plan-flow goal G5 (ledger id "G5", goals ledger, milestone M19), status `clarifying`, no questions filed yet. FRESH goal — run your DEFAULT single-planner state-machine step: ground yourself read-only in the goal's description and the actual repo, then file the FIRST batch of CLARIFYING QUESTIONS (the design decisions to pin before a task DAG can be planned) and return `awaiting-answers`. Do NOT emit a plan or lock a decision — clarify-first round.

The goal (G5): "Optional DNS (hostname) concentrator endpoints" — let an edge dial a concentrator by NAME, mainly to support a concentrator with a changing/DDNS IP. The goal's `description` carries the full motivation, chosen direction (opt-in, default IP-only; resolve+reconcile like tolerant-startup; a re-resolution loop that repoints via SetPeerRemote; the DPI plaintext-DNS leak trade-off), the known refactor surface, invariants, a pre-seeded OPEN QUESTIONS list, non-goals, and testing direction. READ IT.

Ground against the real code before asking — the goal cites specific spots to verify: internal/config/config.go:495 (resolveEndpoints uses netip.ParseAddrPort — rejects hostnames), internal/bind/multipath.go:1327 (ParseEndpoint also netip.ParseAddrPort), internal/metrics/server.go:132/151 (existing DNS resolution for the loopback check — proves the codebase can resolve DNS), internal/device/failover.go + the T55 reconcile loop + startHubFailover (the existing repoint/re-resolution-shaped machinery and SetPeerRemote), internal/bind (tolerant-startup / deferred-path model from W1 to mirror for deferred resolution), docs/design.md (the DPI-resistance thesis + invariants). Confirm the premises hold so your questions are grounded, not speculative.

The goal already enumerates the key open questions (default on/off; hard-resolve-at-load vs defer-and-reconcile; re-resolution trigger + cadence + reuse-SetPeerRemote; multi-record A/AAAA handling + v4/v6 preference; resolver privacy — system vs DoH/DoT vs documented-DPI-tradeoff + the security acceptance target; hub-failover interaction with hostname endpoints + how re-resolution composes with a failover switch; config surface — new hostname field vs overloading endpoint/endpoints + validation). Turn these into concrete, decision-forcing clarifying questions, and add any you find once grounded. File them per your normal rules and return the token.

End with your `### Session summary`.

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.

- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.

- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.

Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

{"item":{"id":"G5","milestoneId":"M19","status":"clarifying","fields":{"title":"Optional DNS (hostname) concentrator endpoints","description":"GOAL: optional DNS (hostname) resolution for the edge's concentrator endpoint / ordered endpoints list, so an edge can dial a concentrator BY NAME — principally to support a concentrator with NO static IP (DDNS / a cloud instance whose public IP changes).

MOTIVATION. Today endpoint/endpoints are IP:port literals ONLY — enforced by netip.ParseAddrPort in config.Peer.resolveEndpoints (internal/config/config.go:495) and Multipath.ParseEndpoint (internal/bind/multipath.go:1327), both of which reject hostnames. This is a DELIBERATE boundary, not a capability gap: the codebase already resolves DNS elsewhere (internal/metrics/server.go:132,151 resolves a hostname listen address to enforce loopback). It is purely EDGE-side (the concentrator has no endpoint — it learns edges dynamically), and largely orthogonal to the multi-peer concentrator goal (G4).

CHOSEN DIRECTION (to confirm/refine in planning): OPT-IN DNS endpoints, default IP-only. Three things must be solved to make it useful and safe:
- (a) RESOLUTION TIMING: the datapath sends to a concrete netip.AddrPort (per-path remote), so a hostname must resolve to an IP before any packet egresses. A one-time resolve at config load handles a static IP behind a name — but that is no better than putting the IP in the config. So resolution should DEFER/RECONCILE (like the W1 tolerant-startup model) rather than hard-fail config.Load when the resolver/network is not ready at boot.
- (b) RE-RESOLUTION (the actual value): WireGuard resolves an endpoint ONCE and never re-resolves (wg-quick re-resolves externally via a timer). To support a CHANGING concentrator IP, add a re-resolution loop that, on TTL / liveness-loss, re-resolves the hostname and — if the IP changed — repoints the bond via the EXISTING bind.Multipath.SetPeerRemote path (the same machinery T57 hub-failover uses). Without this loop, DNS is strictly worse than an IP literal (a startup dependency for no benefit).
- (c) DPI METADATA LEAK: wanbond's thesis is a high-entropy, unfingerprintable wire (amnezia obfuscation, no fixed offsets). A plaintext DNS query for the concentrator hostname is a cleartext, on-path-observable, PRE-tunnel signal that reveals a nameable/blocklistable host + timing over an unprotected channel — a real DPI-resistance regression. So DNS must be opt-in (default off), and the plan should decide DoH/DoT support vs system resolver vs a documented DPI trade-off.

KNOWN REFACTOR SURFACE: internal/config (resolveEndpoints: accept + resolve or defer hostnames; keep the resolved netip.AddrPort shape T57 consumes; a raw-hostname field alongside; validation; keep IP-literal endpoints byte-for-byte behavior-identical), internal/bind/multipath.go (ParseEndpoint; re-resolution repoints via SetPeerRemote), internal/device/device.go (a re-resolution loop wired like startHubFailover / the T55 reconcile loop; startup tolerance for a not-yet-resolvable name), possibly internal/telemetry (trigger re-resolve on liveness loss). Compose with T57 hub-failover (each ordered endpoint could itself be a hostname).

INVARIANTS / CONSTRAINTS TO PRESERVE: A1 one-virtual-endpoint-per-peer; the tolerant-startup model (do not hard-fail boot on a transient resolver outage); the DPI-resistance thesis (default IP-only; DNS opt-in); IP-literal endpoints stay byte-for-byte behavior-identical (no regression for existing configs); resolution never blocks the send hot path (resolve off-path, cache the result).

OPEN QUESTIONS FOR CLARIFICATION (planner should ask before designing):
- Default posture: DNS opt-in with default IP-only (recommended), or on by default?
- Load-time behavior: hard-resolve-at-config-load, or defer-and-reconcile (recommended, mirrors W1 tolerant startup) when a name is not yet resolvable?
- Re-resolution trigger + cadence: honor DNS TTL, a fixed poll interval, on-liveness-loss, or a combination? What repoints the bond — reuse SetPeerRemote?
- Multi-record: a name can resolve to several A/AAAA records — pick first, expand into extra ordered endpoints (feeding hub-failover), or happy-eyeballs? IPv4/IPv6 preference?
- Resolver privacy: system resolver (leaks plaintext DNS), DoH/DoT (more machinery, still leaks SNI/timing), or document the DPI trade-off and leave it to the operator? What is the security acceptance target?
- Hub-failover interaction: may the ordered endpoints list mix hostnames and IP literals? How does re-resolution compose with an in-progress hub-failover switch (which endpoint is re-resolved, and does a re-resolve override a failover or vice-versa)?
- Config surface: new raw-hostname field vs overloading endpoint/endpoints to accept either form; how validation distinguishes and reports.

NON-GOALS: SRV records / service discovery; DNS on the concentrator side (it has no endpoint); any change to the obfuscation wire format; the edge-side simultaneous multi-concentrator aggregation feature (separate).

TESTING DIRECTION: unit — resolveEndpoints resolves a hostname via an injected resolver seam and DEFERS (not hard-fails) on lookup failure; a re-resolution unit test repoints via SetPeerRemote when the injected resolver returns a changed IP (injected resolver + fake clock). netns e2e — an edge dials the concentrator BY NAME (a hosts-file / local resolver entry), the concentrator's IP changes mid-session, and the edge re-resolves and reconnects with the tunnel surviving. Report-only realhosts extension if feasible, per the M10/Q12 discipline."},"createdAt":"2026-07-13T21:17:03.895Z","updatedAt":"2026-07-13T21:17:03.895Z","author":"opus-4.8[1m]","session":"45fdce95-2af6-42cd-8ddd-0c9faabc56ef"}}

459		// EndpointsRaw is the ORDERED list of concentrator (peer) endpoint address:port
460		// strings for the edge-side hub-failover config surface (Q18): index 0 is the
461		// active/primary concentrator, the rest are ordered standbys. Edge-only; a
462		// concentrator declaring it is a config error (validate). Mutually exclusive
463		// with the legacy Endpoint field.
464		EndpointsRaw []string `toml:"endpoints"`
465		// Endpoints is EndpointsRaw (or the single legacy Endpoint, as a one-element
466		// list) parsed to netip.AddrPort and de-duplicated; populated by
467		// resolveEndpoints in normalize(). This is the shape T57's hub-failover
468		// switch consumes: Endpoints[0] is the active concentrator, Endpoints[1:]
469		// are the ordered standbys to fail over to in order when all paths to the
470		// active one are down.
471		Endpoints []netip.AddrPort `toml:"-"`
472		// AllowedIPs are the CIDR ranges routed to this peer.

476	// resolveEndpoints canonicalizes the peer's endpoint config into the ordered
477	// Endpoints list (Q18): the legacy single Endpoint field and the new ordered
478	// EndpointsRaw list are mutually exclusive input forms, but a bare `endpoint =
479	// "..."` is normalized here to a ONE-ELEMENT Endpoints list — so it stays
480	// behavior-identical to the pre-T54 single-defaultRemote config surface. Each
481	// entry must parse as netip.AddrPort ("host:port"), the same format
482	// bind.Multipath.ParseEndpoint requires of the UAPI endpoint string at
483	// runtime, and duplicates within the list are rejected.
484	func (p *Peer) resolveEndpoints() error {
485		if p.Endpoint != "" && len(p.EndpointsRaw) > 0 {
486			return errors.New("endpoint and endpoints are mutually exclusive; endpoint is the single-entry legacy form of endpoints")
487		}
488		raw := p.EndpointsRaw
489		if p.Endpoint != "" {
490			raw = []string{p.Endpoint}
491		}
492		seen := make(map[netip.AddrPort]struct{}, len(raw))
493		endpoints := make([]netip.AddrPort, 0, len(raw))
494		for _, s := range raw {
495			ap, err := netip.ParseAddrPort(s)
496			if err != nil {
497				return fmt.Errorf("invalid endpoint %q: %w", s, err)
498			}
499			if _, dup := seen[ap]; dup {
500				return fmt.Errorf("duplicate endpoint %q", s)
501			}
502			seen[ap] = struct{}{}
503			endpoints = append(endpoints, ap)
504		}
505		p.Endpoints = endpoints
506		return nil
507	}

509	// Amnezia holds the amneziawg-go obfuscation parameters. They must match on both
510	// ends for the handshake to succeed; they are defense-in-depth only.
511	//
512	// The block is "all-or-nothing": either every junk/size knob is left zero (plain
513	// WireGuard) or the whole obfuscation set (jc, jmin, jmax, s1, s2) is specified.
514	// A PARTIAL block silently produces an obfuscation profile the two ends cannot
515	// agree on, so validate rejects it at load (defect D1). The four magic headers
516	// (h1-h4) default to the standard message-type values 1..4 when omitted.
517	type Amnezia struct {
518		Jc   int    `toml:"jc"`
519		Jmin int    `toml:"jmin"`
520		Jmax int    `toml:"jmax"`
521		S1   int    `toml:"s1"`
522		S2   int    `toml:"s2"`
523		H1   uint32 `toml:"h1"`
524		H2   uint32 `toml:"h2"`
525		H3   uint32 `toml:"h3"`
526		H4   uint32 `toml:"h4"`
527	}

530	// response, cookie-reply, and transport. amneziawg-go treats any magic header <= 4
531	// as "use the standard type", so 1..4 is the canonical "headers not obfuscated"
532	// profile. wanbond emits them explicitly (rather than 0) so a configured amnezia
533	// block always carries a complete, self-consistent set of magic headers.
534	var defaultMagicHeaders = [4]uint32{1, 2, 3, 4}
535	
536	// Configured reports whether the amnezia block carries any obfuscation parameter.
537	// An all-zero block leaves the engine in plain WireGuard mode.
538	func (a Amnezia) Configured() bool {
539		return a.Jc != 0 || a.Jmin != 0 || a.Jmax != 0 || a.S1 != 0 || a.S2 != 0 ||
540			a.H1 != 0 || a.H2 != 0 || a.H3 != 0 || a.H4 != 0
541	}

1290	// (T24): the send-side parity overhead and the receive-side recovery outcome. It
1291	// follows the PathSnapshots discipline — grab the send/receive state pointers under
1292	// m.mu, then read the decoder's stats OUTSIDE m.mu (the decoder has its own mutex) —
1293	// so the scrape never blocks an in-flight Send. All zero when FEC is disabled or the
1294	// bind is closed.
1295	func (m *Multipath) FECSnapshot() FECStats {
1296		m.mu.Lock()
1297		fs := m.fecSend
1298		fr := m.fecRecv.Load()
1299		m.mu.Unlock()
1300	
1301		var out FECStats
1302		if fs != nil {
1303			out.DataFrames = fs.dataFrames.Load()
1304			out.DataBytes = fs.dataBytes.Load()
1305			out.ParityFrames = fs.parityFrames.Load()
1306			out.ParityBytes = fs.parityBytes.Load()
1307		}
1308		if fr != nil {
1309			if fr.connLoss != nil {
1310				out.ResidualLoss = fr.connLoss.Loss()
1311			}
1312			// Recovered is the HONEST delivered count (frames placed ahead of the release
1313			// point), NOT the decoder's raw reconstruction count — a frame rebuilt after the
1314			// resequencer skipped its gap is reconstructed but never delivered, so counting it
1315			// would overstate recovery on /metrics. Unrecoverable is the decoder's repair-
1316			// failure count (groups evicted still incomplete).
1317			out.Recovered = fr.deliveredRecovered.Load()
1318			out.Unrecoverable = fr.stats().Unrecoverable
1319		}
1320		return out
1321	}

1323	// ParseEndpoint records the peer's wireguard endpoint as the default per-path
1324	// remote and returns the single virtual endpoint. It may run before Open (the
1325	// engine applies UAPI config before binding), so the parsed address is stashed
1326	// and also applied to any already-open path lacking its own dest_addr.
1327	func (m *Multipath) ParseEndpoint(s string) (Endpoint, error) {
1328		ap, err := netip.ParseAddrPort(s)
1329		if err != nil {
1330			return nil, err
1331		}
1332		m.mu.Lock()
1333		defer m.mu.Unlock()
1334		m.defaultRemote, m.hasDefaultRemote = ap, true
1335		if !m.virt.dstValid() {
1336			m.virt.setDst(ap)
1337		}
1338		for _, ps := range m.paths {
1339			if _, ok := ps.getRemote(); !ok {
1340				ps.setRemote(ap)
1341			}
1342		}
1343		return m.virt, nil
1344	}

1346	// SetPeerRemote repoints EVERY path's wire remote — and the default remote seeded onto
1347	// paths without their own dest_addr — at ap, the concentrator endpoint the edge now
1348	// sends to. It is the edge-side HUB-FAILOVER switch (T57): when every path's liveness to
1349	// the active concentrator is DOWN (hub loss), the device advances to the next ordered
1350	// peer endpoint (config.Peer.Endpoints) and calls this so every subsequent DATA and
1351	// PROBE frame egresses toward the STANDBY concentrator on every path.
1352	//
1353	// It repoints UNIFORMLY — overriding a path's own configured dest_addr too — because a
1354	// concentrator switch retargets the peer for the whole bond: the ordered endpoint list
1355	// is a per-CONCENTRATOR list (one address per hub), so on failover every path must reach
1356	// the same standby hub. (A per-path dest_addr addresses the ACTIVE concentrator's
1357	// multi-address fronting; a hub switch supersedes it. All hubs in the ordered set share
1358	// the peer's single WireGuard static key, so the same session identity re-handshakes
1359	// against whichever hub is active.)
1360	//
1361	// It does NOT touch the engine's single virtual endpoint (invariant A1): the engine
1362	// still sees one peer, and only the per-path fan-out BENEATH it is repointed — no
1363	// per-packet endpoint churn reaches the engine. Fresh-session semantics (dropping the
1364	// old hub's keypairs, no hub-to-hub state handoff) and the re-handshake initiation are
1365	// the device's job, driven right after this call.
1366	//
1367	// Concurrency mirrors ParseEndpoint: it takes m.mu to publish the new default and
1368	// repoint each path's remote (each ps.setRemote takes the path's own mutex). It is
1369	// safe on a CLOSED bind (no paths) — the new default remote is still recorded, so the
1370	// next Open seeds fresh paths with it.
1371	func (m *Multipath) SetPeerRemote(ap netip.AddrPort) {
1372		m.mu.Lock()
1373		m.defaultRemote, m.hasDefaultRemote = ap, true
1374		for _, ps := range m.paths {
1375			ps.setRemote(ap)
1376		}
1377		rq := m.resequencer.Load()
1378		m.mu.Unlock()

1	package device
2	
3	import (
4		"net/netip"
5		"sync"
6		"time"
7	
8		awgdevice "github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/device"
9	
10		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/bind"
11		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
12		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
13		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
14	)
15	
16	// hubFailoverSettle is the dwell a newly-selected concentrator endpoint is given to
17	// prove itself LIVE before the controller may advance again. It exists to keep a
18	// STILL-DOWN reading — caused merely by echoes not having returned from the just-
19	// selected hub yet — from skipping straight past a healthy standby.
20	//
21	// It must comfortably exceed the liveness UP-recovery latency: a DOWN path comes UP
22	// after telemetry.DefaultUpSuccesses (3) probe echoes at telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval
23	// (200ms) ≈ 600ms once probes reach a reachable hub. At 3s the dwell clears that with a
24	// wide margin for jitter/RTT, and also bounds the re-advance cadence while a whole hub
25	// fleet is down to at most one switch (one fresh handshake) per 3s. Hub loss is a
26	// coarse, rare event (a concentrator down), distinct from the sub-second PER-PATH
27	// failover the schedulers own, so a switch latency on this scale is acceptable.
28	const hubFailoverSettle = 3 * time.Second
29	
30	// hubHealth is one path's liveness verdict, the read side of the T13 telemetry state
31	// machine (*telemetry.Prober satisfies it). The hub-failover controller reads exactly
32	// the same up/down verdict the schedulers select on, so "hub loss" is derived from the
33	// very liveness plane per-path failover already runs on — no second detector.
34	type hubHealth interface {
35		State() telemetry.PathState
36	}
37	
38	// peerRemote repoints the whole bond's wire remote at a new concentrator endpoint
39	// (*bind.Multipath satisfies it via SetPeerRemote). It is the action half of a hub
40	// switch on the DATA/PROBE plane; the re-handshake is separate (rehandshake).
41	type peerRemote interface {
42		SetPeerRemote(ap netip.AddrPort)
43	}
44	
45	// rehandshake drops the current WireGuard session and initiates a FRESH one against the
46	// peer's static key — no hub-to-hub state handoff. The device wires it to the engine
47	// peer (expire keypairs + send handshake initiation); a test injects a counter.
48	type rehandshake func()
49	
50	// hubFailover is the edge-side active-standby concentrator failover controller (Q18/T57).
51	// It watches the per-path liveness plane and, when EVERY path to the currently-active
52	// concentrator is DOWN simultaneously (HUB LOSS — distinct from a single path failing,
53	// which the schedulers already absorb), advances to the next endpoint in the ordered
54	// concentrator list, repoints every path's remote at it, and initiates a fresh WG
55	// re-handshake. It re-arms against the new endpoint and, if that one is also fully down,
56	// advances again.
57	//
58	// End-of-list policy: WRAP (round-robin modulo the list length). Once the last standby
59	// is exhausted the controller cycles back to index 0 and keeps retrying every endpoint
60	// in order. Wrap is chosen over stop because it preserves availability — a concentrator
61	// that RECOVERS earlier in the list is retried and settled on within one full cycle,
62	// whereas stopping at the last endpoint would strand the edge on a dead hub even after
63	// endpoint 0 came back. The settle dwell bounds the churn to one switch (one handshake)
64	// per hubFailoverSettle while the whole fleet is down, so the round-robin is a slow,
65	// bounded retry, not a storm.
66	//
67	// GUARD (must-hold invariant): a SINGLE-endpoint list takes NO failover action at all —
68	// check returns immediately, so the remote is never repointed and no re-handshake is
69	// initiated. A one-concentrator deployment therefore behaves EXACTLY as it did before
70	// T57 (the endpoints-list "list" is the one-element normalization of the legacy single
71	// endpoint form, per T54).
72	type hubFailover struct {
73		// endpoints is the ORDERED concentrator endpoint list (config.Peer.Endpoints):
74		// index 0 = active/primary at boot, the rest ordered standbys. Immutable after
75		// construction. IP:port only — no DNS resolution (the T54 constraint).
76		endpoints []netip.AddrPort
77		// health is the per-path liveness, one entry per configured path. Immutable after
78		// construction (the prober set is fixed for the tunnel's life).
79		health []hubHealth
80	
81		remote      peerRemote
82		rehandshake rehandshake
83		clock       telemetry.Clock
84		// settle is the dwell a freshly-selected endpoint gets before another advance is
85		// allowed (hubFailoverSettle in production; a test injects its own).
86		settle time.Duration
87		log    log.Logger
88	
89		mu sync.Mutex
90		// idx is the ACTIVE endpoint index (0 at boot). Guarded by mu.
91		idx int
92		// lastSwitch is when the active endpoint was last (re)selected — initialized to
93		// construction time so endpoint 0 gets the SAME settle grace at boot that a switched
94		// endpoint gets, preventing a boot-time false failover while probers are still DOWN
95		// before the first echo returns. Guarded by mu.
96		lastSwitch time.Time
97	}
98	
99	// newHubFailover builds the controller over the ordered endpoint list and the per-path
100	// liveness set. It is a pure constructor (no goroutine, no engine dependency beyond the
101	// injected collaborators) so its decision logic is unit-testable against a fake clock,
102	// fake health, and fake remote/rehandshake.
103	func newHubFailover(endpoints []netip.AddrPort, health []hubHealth, remote peerRemote, rh rehandshake, clock telemetry.Clock, settle time.Duration, lg log.Logger) *hubFailover {
104		return &hubFailover{
105			endpoints:   endpoints,
106			health:      health,
107			remote:      remote,
108			rehandshake: rh,
109			clock:       clock,
110			settle:      settle,
111			lastSwitch:  clock.Now(),
112			log:         lg,
113		}
114	}
115	
116	// allDownLocked reports HUB LOSS: every path's liveness to the active concentrator is
117	// DOWN simultaneously. An empty health set is NOT hub loss (there is nothing to declare
118	// dead — a bind without the probe transport never drives this). Caller holds mu.
119	func (h *hubFailover) allDownLocked() bool {
120		if len(h.health) == 0 {
121			return false
122		}
123		for _, hp := range h.health {
124			if hp.State() != telemetry.StateDown {
125				return false
126			}
127		}
128		return true
129	}
130	
131	// check is one failover-evaluation step: on confirmed hub loss (and once the active
132	// endpoint's settle dwell has elapsed) it advances to the next endpoint, repoints the
133	// bond's remote, and initiates a fresh re-handshake. It is idempotent and cheap in the
134	// steady state (any path up, or a single-endpoint list) — a length check plus a liveness
135	// sweep — so a periodic loop may call it at the probe cadence.
136	func (h *hubFailover) check() {
137		h.mu.Lock()
138		defer h.mu.Unlock()
139	
140		// GUARD: a single-endpoint (or empty) list takes NO failover action — no advance, no
141		// remote repoint, no re-handshake — so a one-concentrator deployment is byte-for-byte
142		// the pre-T57 behaviour.
143		if len(h.endpoints) < 2 {
144			return
145		}
146		if !h.allDownLocked() {
147			return
148		}
149		// Settle dwell: give the currently-active endpoint time for its liveness to recover
150		// before advancing, so a not-yet-echoed (transiently DOWN) healthy endpoint is not
151		// skipped. Applies at boot (endpoint 0) and after every switch.
152		now := h.clock.Now()
153		if now.Sub(h.lastSwitch) < h.settle {
154			return
155		}
156	
157		prev := h.idx
158		h.idx = (h.idx + 1) % len(h.endpoints)
159		next := h.endpoints[h.idx]
160		h.lastSwitch = now
161	
162		// Repoint the DATA/PROBE plane FIRST so probes immediately start re-arming detection
163		// against the new endpoint, then initiate the fresh WG re-handshake toward it.
164		h.remote.SetPeerRemote(next)
165		if h.rehandshake != nil {
166			h.rehandshake()
167		}
168		h.log.Warn("hub failover: all paths to active concentrator down; switched endpoint and re-handshaked",
169			"from_index", prev, "to_index", h.idx, "to_endpoint", next.String(), "endpoints", len(h.endpoints))
170	}
171	
172	// deviceRehandshake returns a rehandshake bound to the engine peer identified by pk: it
173	// EXPIRES the peer's current keypairs (dropping the old hub's session — a fresh session
174	// with NO hub-to-hub state handoff) and sends a fresh handshake initiation, which the
175	// Bind fans out to the just-repointed standby endpoint. All hubs in the ordered set
176	// share the peer's single WireGuard static key, so the SAME peer identity re-handshakes
177	// against whichever hub is now active. A peer the engine cannot resolve (never configured
178	// — impossible for the edge's sole peer) yields a no-op so the loop never dereferences
179	// nil. This is the ONLY engine-peer coupling the failover path takes; it stays in the
180	// device package alongside the rest of the engine wiring.
181	func deviceRehandshake(dev *awgdevice.Device, pk config.Key) rehandshake {
182		key := awgdevice.NoisePublicKey(pk.Bytes())
183		return func() {
184			peer := dev.LookupPeer(key)
185			if peer == nil {
186				return
187			}
188			// Drop the current session so the re-handshake starts a FRESH keypair (no hub-to-hub
189			// handoff); ExpireCurrentKeypairs also backdates lastSentHandshake so the immediately
190			// following initiation is not suppressed by the RekeyTimeout guard.
191			peer.ExpireCurrentKeypairs()
192			_ = peer.SendHandshakeInitiation(false)
193		}
194	}
195	
196	// startHubFailover builds and starts the edge-side hub-failover monitor for cfg's
197	// concentrator peer, or returns a NO-OP stopper when hub failover does not apply: the
198	// concentrator role (it roams edges dynamically and has no endpoint list), a bind without
199	// the probe transport (no probers → no liveness plane), or no peer carrying an ordered
200	// (>= 2) endpoint list (a single-concentrator deployment, which the GUARD keeps
201	// behaviour-identical to pre-T57). The edge bonds every path to a SINGLE concentrator, so
202	// the whole per-path prober set IS the liveness of the paths to that concentrator (hub
203	// loss = every one DOWN); the controller drives the first peer carrying an ordered
204	// endpoint list. The returned stopper is what device.Close invokes to halt the loop.
205	func startHubFailover(cfg *config.Config, mp *bind.Multipath, probers []*telemetry.Prober, dev *awgdevice.Device, lg log.Logger) func() {
206		if cfg.Role != config.RoleEdge || len(probers) == 0 {
207			return func() {}
208		}
209		for _, peer := range cfg.WireGuard.Peers {
210			if len(peer.Endpoints) < 2 {
211				continue
212			}
213			health := make([]hubHealth, len(probers))
214			for i, pr := range probers {
215				health[i] = pr
216			}
217			ctrl := newHubFailover(
218				peer.Endpoints, health, mp,
219				deviceRehandshake(dev, peer.PublicKey),
220				telemetry.SystemClock{}, hubFailoverSettle,
221				lg.Component("hubfailover"),
222			)
223			// Poll at the probe cadence: check is cheap (a length check plus a liveness sweep)
224			// and the settle dwell bounds actual switches, so a responsive poll only tightens
225			// detection latency without churning the remote.
226			return ctrl.startHubFailoverLoop(telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval)
227		}
228		return func() {}
229	}
230	
231	// startHubFailoverLoop launches the failover-evaluation goroutine: it calls check every
232	// interval until the returned stopper is invoked (idempotent). It mirrors the bind's
233	// StartProbeLoop/StartReconcileLoop lifecycle glue — a wall-clock ticker driving a
234	// decision function whose every timing choice runs through the injected Clock, so a test
235	// drives check directly against a fake clock and never starts this goroutine. It is a
236	// no-op (no-op stopper) for a single-endpoint list (nothing to fail over to) or a
237	// non-positive interval.
238	func (h *hubFailover) startHubFailoverLoop(interval time.Duration) (stop func()) {
239		if len(h.endpoints) < 2 || interval <= 0 {
240			return func() {}
241		}
242		done := make(chan struct{})
243		go func() {
244			ticker := time.NewTicker(interval)
245			defer ticker.Stop()
246			for {
247				select {
248				case <-done:
249					return
250				case <-ticker.C:
251					h.check()
252				}
253			}
254		}()
255		var once sync.Once
256		return func() { once.Do(func() { close(done) }) }
257	}

# wanbond design

This document explains wanbond's architecture and, specifically, **exactly what
we built on top of the amneziawg-go WireGuard engine**. For setup and operation
see [install.md](install.md); for the front-door overview see the
[README](../README.md).

## Thesis

> Keep the WireGuard engine **unmodified**. Put **all** bonding logic in a custom
> `conn.Bind` beneath it, operating only on opaque, already-encrypted datagrams.

We embed [amneziawg-go](https://github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go) as a library
and use it exactly as intended — for TUN management, the Noise handshake, AEAD
encryption, key rotation (rekey), endpoint roaming, and keepalives. We add
**nothing** inside the engine. Everything wanbond does — multipath scheduling,
outer-frame obfuscation, forward error correction, receive resequencing, and
per-path telemetry — lives in an implementation of the engine's `conn.Bind`
transport interface, which the engine drives for every packet it sends and
receives.

This gives a clean separation: WireGuard owns confidentiality, integrity, and
authenticity of the *payload*; wanbond owns *delivery* across multiple lossy
paths, plus outer obfuscation. The Bind never inspects plaintext — it moves
opaque ciphertext datagrams.

## Why amneziawg-go (and not plain wireguard-go)

The architecture decision is closed: amneziawg-go over plain wireguard-go,
kcp-go, or quic-go. The reason is **DPI resistance** (requirement 6). AmneziaWG
adds configurable obfuscation to the WireGuard wire — junk packets (`jc`, `jmin`,
`jmax`), handshake junk prefixes (`s1`, `s2`), and custom magic headers
(`h1`–`h4`) — as *defense-in-depth* beneath our own outer obfuscation codec.
Using the engine as-is means WireGuard's battle-tested crypto and roaming come
for free while the obfuscation knobs are available when configured.

**Fork-lag hedge.** amneziawg-go is a fork of wireguard-go and can lag upstream
security/perf fixes. We contain that risk: the entire dependency on the engine's
`conn` package is isolated to **one file**, `internal/bind/bind.go`, via type
aliases (`Bind = conn.Bind`, `Endpoint = conn.Endpoint`,
`ReceiveFunc = conn.ReceiveFunc`). The `conn.Bind`/`conn.Endpoint` contracts are
byte-identical between the two forks, so swapping back to upstream wireguard-go
(dropping obfuscation) touches only that file.

## The data path

```
                          EDGE                                     CONCENTRATOR
   ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐      ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  applications / kernel routing         │      │       kernel routing / NAT onward      │
   │                 │                       │      │                 ▲                       │
   │           ┌─────▼──────┐  TUN           │      │           ┌─────┴──────┐  TUN           │
   │           │ WireGuard  │  (amneziawg-go)│      │           │ WireGuard  │                │
   │           │  engine    │  Noise/AEAD    │      │           │  engine    │                │
   │           └─────┬──────┘  rekey/roam    │      │           └─────▲──────┘                │
   │   opaque encrypted datagrams  │         │      │       opaque encrypted datagrams        │
   │           ┌─────▼──────────────────────┐│      │┌──────────────────────┴─────┐          │
   │           │   wanbond conn.Bind        ││      ││   wanbond conn.Bind        │          │
   │           │  ┌───────────────────────┐ ││      ││  ┌───────────────────────┐ │          │
   │  send ───►│  │ scheduler (sched)     │ ││      ││  │ resequencer (reseq)   │ │──► recv  │

280	const maxFECShards = 256
281	
282	// FEC configures the fixed-ratio Reed-Solomon forward-error-correction plane (T24).
283	// It is DISABLED by default (like pacing), so an existing config with no [fec] block
284	// runs the datapath exactly as before — FEC transparent, no parity on the wire. When
285	// enabled, each group of DataShards (K) inner datagrams is protected by ParityShards
286	// (M) parity frames, letting the receiver reconstruct up to M lost data frames per
287	// group at a fixed M/K parity overhead.
288	type FEC struct {
289		// Enabled turns the FEC plane on. When false every other field is ignored and the
290		// datapath carries no parity.
291		Enabled bool `toml:"enabled"`
292		// DataShards is K: the number of inner datagrams grouped before parity is emitted.
293		// Must be >= 1 when enabled.
294		DataShards int `toml:"data_shards"`
295		// ParityShards is M: the parity frames emitted per group and the maximum number of
296		// per-group data losses the receiver can recover. Must be >= 1 when enabled. In
297		// adaptive mode it is the CEILING (and the receiver's decoder cardinality) — the
298		// controller drives the per-group parity in [0,ParityShards] to track measured loss.
299		ParityShards int `toml:"parity_shards"`
300		// Deadline bounds grouping latency: a partially-filled group is flushed (parity
301		// emitted over its current data frames) once this much time has elapsed since its
302		// first frame. Defaults to defaultFECDeadline when enabled and left zero; must be
303		// > 0 after defaulting.
304		Deadline time.Duration `toml:"deadline"`
305		// Adaptive opts the send-side FEC into the closed-loop controller (T27/T29): the
306		// per-group parity count tracks the measured per-path loss instead of standing at
307		// the fixed ParityShards ratio, so a clean path spends near-zero overhead while a
308		// lossy one is masked. It is OFF by default, so an existing [fec] block keeps the
309		// fixed-ratio behaviour (T24) byte-for-byte. When true, ParityShards is reinterpreted
310		// as the controller's parity ceiling (see above); the receiver is unchanged because a
311		// group coded with fewer parity shards decodes against the ParityShards-ceiling codec
312		// unchanged (klauspost's parity is prefix-consistent).
313		Adaptive bool `toml:"adaptive"`
314		// SafetyFactor is the adaptive controller's headroom multiplier over the measured mean
315		// loss the per-group parity is sized to mask (adaptivefec.Config.SafetyFactor). It is
316		// the residual-loss LEVER: the controller sizes M so M/(K+M) >= SafetyFactor*loss, so a
317		// higher factor spends more parity to keep the post-recovery residual under a tighter
318		// bound against binomial per-group variance. Applies only in adaptive mode; defaults to
319		// defaultAdaptiveSafetyFactor (the simulation-proven controller default) when left zero

That informs the config-surface and default-posture questions. Let me wait for the explorer's reconcile-loop findings.

1380		// A hub switch changes the DATA-frame SENDER identity: the standby concentrator is a
1381		// separate process whose outer-seq restarts near 1, far below the release point the
1382		// prior hub's stream advanced the shared resequencer's `next` to. Re-baseline it so the
1383		// standby's FIRST frame (the WG handshake response) re-anchors the release point instead
1384		// of being dropped as a suspect low seq — without this the tunnel never re-establishes
1385		// after failover (defect D32). Done OUTSIDE m.mu (the resequencer has its own mutex;
1386		// never nest it under m.mu). Nil on a closed bind (a resequencer is Stored per Open) —
1387		// the next Open seeds a fresh one whose release point re-pins to its first frame anyway.
1388		if rq != nil {
1389			rq.Rebaseline()
1390		}
1391	}
1392	
1393	// Close tears down every per-path socket and CLEARS the bind's path state so a
1394	// subsequent Open fully rebuilds it. The amneziawg engine drives exactly this
1395	// lifecycle — device.upLocked → BindUpdate → closeBindLocked calls Close() before
1396	// every Open(), and a Down/Up cycles Close after Open — so Close must leave the
1397	// bind reopenable (matching conn.StdNetBind, whose closed state is simply "no
1398	// sockets"). Outstanding receive calls return an error as their socket closes.

content.text: This is a key finding: `SetPeerRemote` triggers `resequencer.Rebaseline()` (defect D32 fix) and hub-failover holds `endpoints []netip.AddrPort` as an **immutable** snapshot. The explorer confirms the T55 model precisely: `deferredPath` + `StartReconcileLoop(bind.DefaultReconcileInterval)` (~1s) retries `ListenUDP` and promotes on success (`internal/bind/reconcile.go:60-124`), and device loops are stored as idempotent stop-closures started at `device.go:248-260` / stopped at `664-682`. A re-resolution loop hooks in identically.

fields.question: Default posture: is DNS/hostname endpoint resolution strictly OPT-IN with default IP-only, and does an existing IP-literal config stay byte-for-byte behavior-identical (no new code path taken when no hostname is configured)?
fields.context: The goal's core DPI-resistance constraint is that a plaintext DNS query is a cleartext, on-path, pre-tunnel signal that names a blocklistable host — a regression against the thesis (design.md 'DPI resistance', requirement 6). This codebase already has a firm idiom for exactly this shape: FEC, pacing, adaptive-FEC, and the amnezia block are all default-off and leave the existing datapath byte-for-byte unchanged when unconfigured (config.go FEC L288, Amnezia L517). Confirming the posture pins whether resolution is gated behind an explicit opt-in and whether the IP-literal parse path (netip.ParseAddrPort at config.go:495, multipath.go:1327) must be preserved untouched for non-hostname configs.
suggestions: Opt-in, default IP-only; IP-literal path untouched (matches FEC/amnezia idiom)
On by default (any endpoint may be a hostname), IP still parsed fast-path
Opt-in AND require an explicit acknowledgement flag for the DPI trade-off
fields.recommendation: Opt-in, default IP-only, IP-literal configs byte-for-byte identical — matches the goal's stated direction and the codebase's established default-off feature-block idiom.

fields.question: Load-time behavior when a configured hostname is NOT yet resolvable at boot (resolver/network not ready): hard-fail config.Load, or DEFER-and-reconcile mirroring the T55 tolerant-startup model — bring the tunnel up on any resolvable/IP endpoints and let a background loop resolve the name later?
fields.context: The T55 tolerant-startup path is a precise, existing template: internal/bind/multipath.go:531-537 defers a path on EADDRNOTAVAIL into m.deferred, and StartReconcileLoop (internal/bind/reconcile.go:60-124, ~1s cadence) retries ListenUDP and promotes on success. An unresolvable hostname at boot is structurally the same situation. The datapath sends to a concrete netip.AddrPort (multipath ParseEndpoint/SetPeerRemote), so a name MUST resolve before any packet egresses on that endpoint — but hard-failing Load would violate the invariant 'do not hard-fail boot on a transient resolver outage'. The decision determines whether resolveEndpoints (config.go:484) may return a config with an as-yet-unresolved hostname placeholder, and whether a deferred-resolution reconcile loop is in scope.
suggestions: Defer-and-reconcile: boot succeeds, background loop resolves later (mirror T55)
Hard-resolve at config.Load, fail boot if any hostname does not resolve
Hybrid: hard-resolve if it is the ONLY/primary endpoint, defer if standbys exist
fields.recommendation: Defer-and-reconcile, mirroring T55: boot tolerantly, resolve off-path, and only start sending on that endpoint once resolved — preserves the tolerant-startup invariant and makes a single-hostname edge boot even before its resolver is up.

fields.question: Re-resolution loop — what TRIGGERS a re-resolve, at what CADENCE, and how does a changed IP repoint the bond? Specifically: (a) honor DNS record TTL, a fixed poll interval, on-liveness-loss, or a combination; (b) reuse bind.Multipath.SetPeerRemote to repoint; (c) suppress a no-op repoint when the resolved IP is UNCHANGED?
fields.context: Re-resolution is the goal's actual value (a static IP behind a name is no better than the IP literal). The repoint machinery already exists: SetPeerRemote (multipath.go:1371) repoints every path's remote AND calls resequencer.Rebaseline() plus (via the hub-failover caller) a fresh WG re-handshake — this is deliberately DISRUPTIVE (defect D32: a hub switch restarts the sender's outer-seq, so the resequencer must re-baseline). Therefore repointing to the SAME IP would needlessly drop the session, so a change-detection guard is essential. Go's net.Resolver does not surface TTL directly (net.LookupIP discards it), so honoring TTL needs a resolver seam that exposes it or a fixed interval. The loop would wire in exactly like startHubFailover (device.go:260) with a stopResolution closure (device.go:664-682). The cadence + trigger choice sizes the reconnect latency after a DDNS IP change versus steady-state resolver load.
suggestions: Fixed poll interval + re-resolve on liveness-loss; reuse SetPeerRemote; skip if IP unchanged
Honor DNS TTL (needs a TTL-exposing resolver seam); re-resolve on expiry
On-liveness-loss ONLY (re-resolve when all paths to the name go DOWN), no timer
fields.recommendation: Fixed poll interval (bounded, e.g. reconcile-cadence-scale) PLUS an immediate re-resolve on liveness-loss, repoint via SetPeerRemote ONLY when the resolved AddrPort actually changed. TTL-honoring is a nice-to-have gated on whether we add a TTL-exposing resolver seam.

fields.question: Multi-record handling: when a hostname resolves to SEVERAL A/AAAA records, do we pick the first, expand them into extra ordered endpoints (feeding hub-failover), or do happy-eyeballs? And what is the IPv4/IPv6 preference?
fields.context: config.Peer.Endpoints is an ordered []netip.AddrPort that T57 hub-failover consumes as active(0)/standbys(1:) with WRAP round-robin (failover.go). A multi-record name maps naturally onto that list — but expanding one name into N ordered endpoints changes failover semantics (a re-resolve could add/remove standbys, and hubFailover holds an IMMUTABLE snapshot of endpoints with an active idx, so a changing record set races that snapshot — see the hub-failover-interaction question). Picking one address is simplest and keeps a 1:1 name->endpoint mapping. IPv4/IPv6 preference matters because a path's local bind family must match the remote family; the send path uses a single netip.AddrPort per path.
suggestions: Pick one (first, or first matching the path's address family); 1:1 name->endpoint
Expand all records into ordered endpoints feeding hub-failover
Happy-eyeballs (race v4/v6, keep the winner)
fields.recommendation: Pick a single address per name (prefer the family that matches available local paths; document the tie-break) for a clean 1:1 name->endpoint mapping. Expanding into the failover list is a larger, separable change with real interaction complexity — defer it unless explicitly wanted.

content.text: Now the remaining four questions: resolver privacy, hub-failover interaction, config surface, and testing acceptance.

fields.question: Resolver privacy: use the SYSTEM resolver (leaks a plaintext DNS query naming the concentrator), DoH/DoT (more machinery; still leaks SNI + timing to the DoH provider), or the system resolver with a DOCUMENTED DPI trade-off left to the operator? And what is the concrete SECURITY ACCEPTANCE TARGET this must meet?
fields.context: This is the crux of the DPI-resistance tension (goal point c). wanbond's thesis is a high-entropy, unfingerprintable wire; a plaintext DNS lookup for the concentrator name is a pre-tunnel cleartext signal that names a blocklistable host and reveals timing over an unprotected channel. DoH/DoT reduce the on-path plaintext but add a TLS client dependency and still leak SNI/timing to (and trust in) the DoH resolver — and the existing p5_dpi_test.go wire audit only governs the TUNNEL wire, not a pre-tunnel resolver. Per this project's operationalism norm the plan needs a TESTABLE acceptance criterion, not 'is private': e.g. 'with DNS opt-in OFF, zero DNS traffic and the wire audit is unchanged'; 'with system resolver, the operator-facing docs state the exact leaked artifact (hostname in cleartext DNS)'. The choice sizes scope enormously (system resolver = small; DoH = a new subsystem).
suggestions: System resolver + explicit documented DPI trade-off (smallest); default-off already contains the leak
System resolver now, design the seam so DoH/DoT can be added later without a datapath change
DoH/DoT in-scope now as a first-class private-resolver option

Design the seam so DoH/DoT is a later drop-in, but do not build it now unless required.

fields.question: Hub-failover interaction: may the ordered endpoints list MIX hostnames and IP literals? And how do re-resolution and an in-progress hub-failover switch COORDINATE — which endpoint does re-resolution target, and does a re-resolve override a failover selection or vice-versa?
fields.context: This is the hardest composition concern and it is concrete, not speculative. hubFailover (internal/device/failover.go:72-97) holds endpoints []netip.AddrPort as an IMMUTABLE snapshot taken at construction, tracks the active idx, and BOTH controllers would call the same bind.Multipath.SetPeerRemote. If a re-resolution loop repoints endpoint[idx]'s AddrPort out from under hubFailover, its snapshot goes stale and the two can fight over the bond's remote (each SetPeerRemote also triggers a resequencer Rebaseline + re-handshake, so a fight = repeated session drops). Options: (a) re-resolution only ever repoints the CURRENTLY-ACTIVE endpoint and defers to failover for selection; (b) make the endpoint list mutable/owned by one coordinator; (c) forbid mixing (a hostname endpoint list is single-entry, so failover and DNS are mutually exclusive per peer). The answer decides whether failover.go's endpoints field must become resolvable/mutable or stays IP-only with DNS handled by a separate single-endpoint path.
suggestions: Allow mixing; ONE coordinator owns endpoint selection, re-resolution only rewrites the active entry's IP and never advances idx
Forbid mixing for v1: a hostname endpoint is single-entry only (DNS XOR multi-endpoint failover per peer)
Allow mixing; make hubFailover's endpoint list mutable and re-resolution updates entries in place under a shared lock
fields.recommendation: For v1, keep them decoupled: re-resolution only ever rewrites the ACTIVE endpoint's resolved IP (never advances the failover idx), and failover continues to own selection. Whether to also FORBID mixing hostnames into a multi-entry list for v1 (simplest, smallest blast radius) is the key sub-decision I need your call on.

fields.question: Config surface: introduce a NEW field for hostname endpoints (e.g. an explicit endpoint_hostname / endpoints_dns), or OVERLOAD the existing endpoint/endpoints fields to accept either an IP:port literal or a host:port name? How should validation distinguish the two forms and report errors?
fields.context: Today Peer has Endpoint (legacy single) and EndpointsRaw (ordered []string), mutually exclusive, both normalized by resolveEndpoints (config.go:452-507) via netip.ParseAddrPort which REJECTS hostnames. Overloading keeps the surface small and lets a hostname sit anywhere in the ordered list, but it silently changes the semantics of the existing fields and couples the opt-in DPI gate to per-value parsing (harder to make DNS explicitly opt-in). A separate field makes the opt-in explicit and greppable and keeps the IP path's parse untouched, at the cost of a third mutually-exclusive input form. Validation must cleanly separate 'is this an IP:port or a host:port' (a bare ':port' split then ParseAddr-vs-treat-as-name) and give a clear error (e.g. reject a hostname when DNS is not opted in).
suggestions: New explicit field/flag (opt-in is greppable; IP path untouched; hostname clearly gated)
Overload endpoint/endpoints to accept either form; a per-entry parse decides IP vs name
Overload, but require a peer-level dns=true flag before any hostname entry is accepted
fields.recommendation: Overload endpoint/endpoints to accept either form BUT gate hostnames behind an explicit per-peer opt-in flag (so the default-off DPI posture stays explicit and greppable, the IP-literal path is unchanged, and a hostname without the flag is a clear validation error). Confirm you prefer this over a wholly separate field.

fields.question: Acceptance bar for 'done': is the required e2e proof a NETNS test where an edge dials the concentrator BY NAME (local hosts-file/resolver entry), the concentrator's IP CHANGES mid-session, and the edge re-resolves and reconnects with the tunnel surviving — plus injected-resolver unit tests for defer-on-lookup-failure and repoint-on-changed-IP? And is a report-only realhosts extension in or out of scope for v1?
fields.context: The goal's testing direction names these, and the harness exists: test/e2e/failover_test.go already exercises a mid-session concentrator switch over netns, and p5_dpi_test.go audits the tunnel wire — so a DNS e2e is an incremental addition, not new infrastructure. The unit seams needed are an injected resolver (like the injected clock telemetry.Clock already used across failover/reconcile tests) and a fake clock for cadence. Pinning the acceptance bar is what makes the plan's tasks testable (operationalism): each task's 'done' should map to one of these observable checks. The realhosts (M10/Q12 report-only) discipline is explicitly 'if feasible' in the goal — I need to know whether to plan it as a task or leave it out of v1.
suggestions: Netns IP-change-survival e2e + injected-resolver/fake-clock unit tests are the v1 bar; realhosts report-only is optional/out
Same, PLUS a realhosts report-only DNS check as an in-scope task
Unit tests only for v1; netns e2e deferred
fields.recommendation: Make the netns 'dial-by-name, IP changes mid-session, tunnel survives' e2e plus injected-resolver unit tests (defer-on-failure; repoint-on-change via SetPeerRemote with a fake clock) the v1 acceptance bar, and treat realhosts report-only as a stretch task included only if it adds signal cheaply. Confirm this bar.

content.text:
Filed 8 clarifying questions (Q29–Q36) under M19, all linked `goals:G5`. The goal stays in `clarifying` — the server won't let it advance while these are open, which correctly gates the next phase.

### Session summary
- **Did:** Grounded read-only against the cited code, then filed the FIRST clarifying-question batch for goal G5 (fresh, no prior questions). No plan emitted, no decision locked.
- **Achieved:** Created questions Q29–Q36 under milestone M19, each linked `goals:G5`, each with grounded context + suggestions + a recommended default. Goal remains `clarifying`. Topics: (Q29) opt-in/default-off posture + IP-literal byte-identity; (Q30) defer-and-reconcile vs hard-resolve at load; (Q31) re-resolution trigger/cadence/repoint + no-op suppression; (Q32) multi-record A/AAAA + v4/v6 preference; (Q33) resolver privacy + a testable security acceptance target; (Q34) hub-failover interaction/coordination; (Q35) config surface (overload vs new field); (Q36) e2e/unit acceptance bar + realhosts scope.
- **Discovered:** Every cited premise holds: `netip.ParseAddrPort` rejects hostnames at both `internal/config/config.go:495` and `internal/bind/multipath.go:1327`; `internal/metrics/server.go:133` proves DNS capability via `net.LookupIP`. Two grounded, non-obvious findings sharpen the questions: (1) `SetPeerRemote` (multipath.go:1371) is deliberately disruptive — it calls `resequencer.Rebaseline()` (defect D32) and the caller triggers a fresh WG re-handshake, so a re-resolution repoint MUST suppress no-op (unchanged-IP) repoints or it drops the session needlessly; (2) `hubFailover` (`internal/device/failover.go:72-97`) holds `endpoints []netip.AddrPort` as an IMMUTABLE snapshot with an active `idx` and also calls `SetPeerRemote`, so an independent re-resolution loop repointing the active endpoint races the failover controller — a real coordination concern (Q34). The T55 tolerant-startup template (`deferredPath` + `StartReconcileLoop` at `internal/bind/reconcile.go:60-124`, ~1s cadence) and the device loop-lifecycle idiom (stop-closures at `device.go:248-260`/`664-682`) give a precise wiring model for the deferred/re-resolution loop.
- **Issues:** None blocking. Answers to Q34 (failover coordination) and Q35 (config surface) most strongly shape the eventual task DAG's blast radius; the goal will re-enter the planner for a grounded plan once the user answers.
